cataloging_subject.label: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The distinct anonymous Middle English Cotton Version of the Book of John Mandeville, uniquely attested in British Library Cotton MS Titus C XVI. This record does not flatten the four passages supplied by Hamelius from Egerton MS 1982 into Cotton-witness evidence, and it does not treat Hamelius's two-volume edition, its 1960-1961 reprint, editorial front matter, introduction, notes, or the University of Michigan encoding as parts of the historical work.

catalog_summary.title: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator-reviser; traditionally attributed to the pseudonymous narrator John Mandeville, whose historical identity is unresolved
catalog_summary.region: England; no more precise place of translation or revision is established

identifiers.scheme: Early English Text Society Original Series

titles.value: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels

notes: Short title of Hamelius's edition; its historical subtitle and Jean d'Outremeuse authorship theory are edition-level claims, not adopted catalog facts.

agents.name: John Mandeville
notes: Narrating persona and traditional author attribution. Current scholarship treats the compiler's identity as unresolved and does not establish a historical knight as eyewitness author.

agents.name: Paul Hamelius
notes: Editor, transcriber, collator, and commentator of the 1919 and 1923 Early English Text Society volumes.

agents.name: D. Ruddy
notes: Contributor of thirty-two labeled notes and four labeled corrections preserved in the U-M encoding; fuller identity was not established in the reviewed sources.
The Cotton Version is based on the English Defective Version and was expanded under influence from the French Insular Version; the reviewed evidence names no translator.
Adaptation and revision distinguish the Cotton Version's expansion and conflation from an unsupported word-for-word translation claim.
The first-person persona claims an English origin and long journey, but this does not establish a historical author or eyewitness biography.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous scribe of Cotton MS Titus C XVI

scope.description: Copying of the unique surviving Cotton witness

notes: Witness-level copying is distinct from creating the Cotton textual version.

scope.description: Cotton-base English reading text, excluding the four explicitly Egerton-supplied passages

notes: The exact manuscript-to-edition scope is recorded by the Cotton transcription relation in the linked lineage manifest.

scope.description: Three short passages on edition pp. 50, 52, and 67 and the large Chapter XV passage printed on pp. 212-217

notes: Four separate passage-scoped lineage edges preserve the exact Egerton source assignments.

scope.description: Comparison with two Brussels French witnesses and Warner's printed Anglo-French text
Edition preface, introduction, textual notes, and source commentary
Thirty-two labeled notes in the encoded edition apparatus
Four edition-layer CORR elements labeled RESP=DR: p. 56/P56.L12 'Þat//Þat' to 'Þat'; p. 69/P69.L5a 'men' to 'then'; p. 87/P87.L12 'thay' to 'the day'; and p. 87/P87.L20 'him' to 'hir'

notes: Orsi dates the expansion of the Defective Version by reference to the Insular Version to this interval. Translation, revision, and witness copying need not be a single event.
The Cotton Version is uniquely attested in this witness.
The prologue and close frame the persona's claimed travel period; this is neither a composition date nor a verified biography.

This is an English-language version in an English manuscript tradition. No reviewed source establishes a more precise place of translation or revision, and witness production place is not silently treated as translator location.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header omits the overwhelmingly primary language; direct inspection establishes Middle English.

form_statements.description: A continuous prose travel compilation. The 133 L elements in the XML all occur in Hamelius's back-matter quotations and are not verse in the historical Cotton work.

genre_statements.label: Travel narrative
notes: The first-person itinerary is a literary compilation, not verified eyewitness travel.

subject_statements.label: Routes to Jerusalem, sacred geography, pilgrimage sites, and relics
The Eastern Mediterranean, Near East, Central Asia, and India
Christianities, Islam, Judaism, other religions, and reported customs
notes: Descriptions reflect medieval Christian polemic and inherited literary sources, not neutral ethnography.

subject_statements.label: Marvelous peoples, animals, islands, and natural wonders
Cosmography, the round earth, geography, and antipodes
The Great Khan, Prester John, and imagined Eastern polities

summaries.value: A Middle English version of the Book of John Mandeville, framed as the recollections and guidance of an English knight travelling from the Holy Land across Asia. The prose combines pilgrimage routes and sacred sites with geography, religions, political powers, natural history, cosmography, and marvels. Modern scholarship treats it as an anonymous literary translation, revision, and compilation rather than reliable evidence of the narrator's claimed eyewitness journey.

Hamelius edited the Cotton witness as a thirty-five-chapter reading text with editorial punctuation and limited emendation. His edition supplies four passages from Egerton MS 1982: three short omissions and a large Chapter XV passage printed in the original volume as an appendix on pp. 212-217. U-M moved that appendix passage into Chapter XV's reading order, retained Hamelius's introduction and notes through volume II p. 151, and omitted the volume II index on pp. 152-189. These edition and encoding interventions remain outside the historical Cotton work's one-item extent.

extent.description: One continuous prose work. The XML BODY represents a prologue and thirty-five editor-labeled chapters in 36 DIV2 elements, 50 P elements, 219 PB elements, and 272 manuscript-folio MILESTONE elements. Its four Egerton-derived SUPPLIED passages, other supplied spans, corrections, pointers, and edition headings are interventions or apparatus rather than additional work items. All 133 L elements and all 1,165 note DIV3 elements occur in back-matter commentary, so the historical work is prose with zero counted verse lines.

content_parts.label: The Cotton Version of Mandeville's Travels
content_parts.summary: The continuous Cotton-version prose, from its pilgrimage prologue through the Holy Land, Asia, marvels, cosmography, Prester John, and the narrator's closing appeal for prayer.

locators.value: volume I pp. 1-211, excluding the four Egerton-supplied passages
BODY: Cot.Prol and Cot.Ch.1-Cot.Ch.35, excluding SUPPLIED[@SOURCE='Eg']

notes: The codex is cataloged as physically intact. The large textual lacuna inherited by the Cotton Version is not evidence that leaves are now missing from the physical manuscript.

content_parts.label: Four Hamelius supplies from Egerton MS 1982
content_parts.summary: Three short passages and one large Chapter XV passage supplied from Egerton to repair lacunae in the edited Cotton reading text.

locators.value: Chapter XI after P49.L32; Chapter XI after P52.L24; Chapter XIII after P67.L26; Chapter XV after P82.L25
short supplies on pp. 50, 52, and 67; appendix pp. 212-217
three passages labeled SOURCE=Eg and one missing-leaves passage labeled SOURCE=Eg

notes: Item count is zero because these are four passage-level editorial supplies within the one cataloged work, not four separately cataloged works.

content_parts.label: Edition and reprint front matter
content_parts.summary: Reprint title and imprint leaves, dedication, Hamelius's preface, contents, and editor's analysis encoded before the historical text.

locators.value: volume I title and preliminary matter [a]-xv

notes: The alternating contents and analysis pages were grouped by DIV1 in the XML, so encoding order differs from physical source-page order.

content_parts.label: Hamelius's volume II foreword, preface, and introduction
content_parts.summary: Editorial history, source study, authorship argument, textual description, and analysis preceding the notes.
notes: Hamelius's Jean d'Outremeuse authorship theory is retained as edition history, not adopted as established authorship.

content_parts.label: Hamelius and Ruddy notes
content_parts.summary: Line-referenced linguistic, textual, historical, and source commentary, including 1,133 Hamelius note divisions and thirty-two Ruddy note divisions.

locators.value: 1,165 note-type DIV3 elements in BACK; nine inline NOTE1 elements occur in the preceding introduction

notes: The volume II index on pp. 152-189 is not encoded.

related_works.title: The Book of John Mandeville
notes: The Book is a multilingual and multiversion textual tradition; this record catalogs only its Cotton Middle English version.

related_works.title: The Defective Version of the Book of John Mandeville
notes: The Cotton Version is based on an English Defective-Version text whose lost exemplar lacked the French original's second quire; this is a textual lacuna, not a claim that Cotton MS Titus C XVI is physically defective today.

related_works.title: The Insular French Version of the Book of John Mandeville
notes: Orsi describes expansion of the English Defective Version under influence from the Insular Version; no reviewed evidence identifies a particular surviving French manuscript as the translator-reviser's exemplar.

related_works.title: The Egerton Version of Mandeville's Travels
notes: Hamelius used Egerton MS 1982 for four passage supplies, but the Egerton Version is not the base text of the cataloged Cotton Version.

evidence.locator: HEADER; FRONT; BODY Cot.Prol and Cot.Ch.1-Cot.Ch.35; BACK
evidence.summary: Identifies U-M item aeh6691, Hamelius's edition, the possible 1960-1961 reprint carrier, embedded availability terms, Cotton and Egerton source labels, editorial responsibilities, and the encoded historical text with front and back matter.

evidence.citation: Namespace-free element, attribute, pointer, page-sequence, and text-scope inspection of General Corpus aeh6691.xml.
evidence.locator: FRONT/BODY/BACK; DIV1-DIV3; P; PB; MILESTONE; SUPPLIED; CORR; PTR; NOTE1; L; LANG attributes
evidence.summary: Finds four front DIV1 elements, two body DIV1 elements with 36 DIV2 divisions, and one back DIV1 containing four DIV2 and 1,172 DIV3 elements. It distinguishes 1,165 note divisions, nine inline introduction notes, 51 PH supplies including four with SOURCE=Eg, 76 corrections (72 PH and four DR), 388 PB markers, 272 manuscript milestones, and 133 editorial back-matter verse lines. It also establishes the reading-order relocation of printed pp. 212-217 into Chapter XV and the omission of the printed index.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Mandeville's Travels: The Cotton Version, item aeh6691.
evidence.locator: Item information, table of contents, full text, stable handle, and rights statement
evidence.summary: Confirms the public 2003 digital item, Hamelius source, displayed work and editorial sections, stable handle, and current Public Domain / No Copyright-United States assertion.

evidence.citation: Paul Hamelius, ed., Mandeville's Travels, Early English Text Society Original Series 153 and 154 (London, 1919 and 1923), combined ORBi scan.
evidence.locator: Volume I title and preface; Cotton-base text pp. 1-211; appendix pp. 212-217; volume II foreword, preface, introduction, notes pp. 23-151, and index pp. 152-189
evidence.summary: The inspected complete original-issue scan establishes the Cotton base, editorial punctuation and restrained emendation, three short and one large Egerton supplies, comparison against two Brussels manuscripts and Warner's printed French text, original source-page order, and the editorial matter omitted or reordered by U-M.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Cotton MS Titus C XVI.
evidence.summary: Confirms the Cotton Version's unique manuscript shelfmark, Middle English language, English production in 1400-1424, physical integrity, support and 133-folio extent, approximate dimensions, current binding, physical-request conditions, and absence of linked digitized content.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Egerton MS 1982.
evidence.summary: Confirms the separate Egerton Middle English manuscript witness and current absence of linked digitized content; Hamelius's edition and XML, rather than this catalog record alone, establish its four passage-level supply roles.

evidence.citation: Tibor Orsi, 'French Phraseological Influence in Mandeville's Travels,' Verbum 5.1 (2003): 221-256.
evidence.summary: Describes the Cotton Version as an English conflation based on the English Defective Version, whose lost source manuscript lacked its second quire, and expanded through reference to the Insular French Version between 1400 and 1425. It does not identify one surviving French manuscript as the translator-reviser's exemplar.

evidence.citation: The Book of John Mandeville, scholarly introduction, Middle English Texts Series Editions.
evidence.summary: Synthesizes current scholarship on the unresolved or pseudonymous authorship, debated French origin and date, major sources, multilingual and multiversion transmission, Cotton and Egerton versions, and the work's travel, pilgrimage, geographical, and marvels genres.

open_questions.question: Which lost or surviving Insular French witness most closely represents the source consulted when the English Defective Version was expanded into the Cotton Version?
next_steps: Consult full modern stemmatic studies and critical apparatus for the Cotton and Defective versions.
Do not identify KBR MS 10420-10425, KBR MS 11141, or Warner's printed text as the translator-reviser's direct exemplar merely because Hamelius later compared them.

open_questions.question: Was U-M item aeh6691 keyed, OCR-derived, or otherwise captured from a particular copy of the 1960-1961 reprint, and what correction workflow was applied?
next_steps: Seek item-specific U-M production records or contact Digital Content and Collections.
Preserve the XML header's qualified 'may have been captured in whole or in part' statement rather than asserting an unverified workflow.

open_questions.question: Which of Hamelius's prologue and thirty-five chapter divisions reflect Cotton manuscript rubrics, and which are modern editorial structuring?
next_steps: Compare the complete physical manuscript or trustworthy facsimile against Hamelius's headings.
Continue treating the one-work prose extent as primary and the encoded chapter hierarchy as edition structure.

notes: The U-M header lists Latin and Greek but omits primary Middle English. Middle English alone is exposed as a work-level language facet; brief embedded Latin formulas and two transliterated Greek quotations remain documented in structural and lineage evidence rather than being promoted to separate work languages.
The four SOURCE=Eg supplies are modeled as passage-level editorial interventions, not evidence that the corresponding wording survives in Cotton MS Titus C XVI.

The XML contains a HEAD TYPE=MS value 'Maundevyll' at the opening, but Hamelius volume I p. 1 visibly prints only the editorial heading 'Mandeville's Travels.' Without direct manuscript or visible source-page confirmation, the XML-only value is treated as an encoding anomaly and not promoted to a work title.
Hamelius's volume II introduction, notes, quoted verse, and 1960-1961 reprint matter remain separate paratext layers and do not inflate the historical work's extent.

Copyright, access, local source-cache delivery, source-page order, and the tension between U-M's current public-domain statement and its embedded 2003 redistribution restriction are recorded in the linked lineage manifest.
